findings.title: Meta-World ML10/ML50 headline benchmark has no code in the repo
findings.quote:
All available environments with corresponding `<Environment>` are listed below:
* *2DMaze* environment: `2dmaze`, [running script](./run-2dmaze.py).
* *3DPickup* environment: `3dpickup`, [running script](./run-3dpickup.py).
* *MujocoCar* environment: `mujococar`, [running script](./run-mujococar.py).
findings.claim: The repo provides run scripts only for 2DMaze, 3DPickup, and MujocoCar; there is no Meta-World environment, run script, or task configuration anywhere in the repository (grep for 'metaworld/ML10/ML50' returns no source hits).
findings.concern: Meta-World ML10-sparse and ML50-sparse are the headline benchmark (leading columns of Tables 1 and 2 and the abstract's claim of validation on 'the representative Meta-World benchmark'), yet none of the code, environments, or task splits needed to produce those numbers is present, so those results cannot be reproduced from this repo.
findings.resolution: Authors: please add the Meta-World sparse-reward environment wrappers, the ML10/ML50 task lists (10/45 train + 5 test tasks), and the run scripts used to produce the Meta-World columns of Tables 1–2.
findings.reason: README.md lines 32–35 list exactly three environments (2DMaze, 3DPickup, MujocoCar); a repo-wide grep for 'metaworld', 'ML10', and 'ML50' returns zero hits, confirming the Meta-World benchmark is entirely absent.

findings.title: None of the 9 compared baselines are implemented in the repo
findings.quote:
## Comparative Evaluation

The comparison of CenRA with several baselines, including the backbone algorithms [DQN](https://www.nature.com/articles/nature14236) (Mnih et al. 2015) for discrete control and [SAC](https://proceedings.mlr.press/v80/haarnoja18b) (Haarnojaet al. 2018) for continuous control, [ReLara](https://proceedings.mlr.press/v235/ma24l.html) (Ma et al. 2024), [PiCor](https://ojs.aaai.org/index.php/AAAI/article/view/25825) (Bai et al. 2023) and [MCAL](https://openreview.net/forum?id=rJvY_5OzoI) (Mysore et al. 2022).
findings.claim: The repo contains only the CenRA framework (CenRA_dis/CenRA_con plus DQNAgent/SACAgent as its own backbones); it does not contain runnable implementations of ReLara, TD-MPC2, CMTA, PiCor, MCAL, PaCo, SC, or SoftModule, which Tables 1–2 report numbers for.
findings.concern: Every baseline column in Tables 1 and 2 (including the central claim 'CenRA consistently outperforms all baselines') is unverifiable from this repo because no baseline training/evaluation code is provided, and the README only links to external papers rather than the code used.
findings.resolution: Authors: please provide the baseline implementations (or exact forks/commit hashes of the CleanRL/official codebases) and the scripts used to run them under the identical sparse-reward tasks.
findings.reason: Repo-wide grep for ReLara, TD-MPC, CMTA, PiCor, MCAL, PaCo, SoftModule returns no source files; only paper links in README.md are present, confirming no baseline implementations exist.

findings.title: No evaluation script computes the '100-episode' returns in Tables 1–3
findings.quote:
def save(self, indicator="best"):
      torch.save(self.actor.state_dict(),
                 os.path.join(self.save_folder, f"ra-actor-{self.exp_name}-{indicator}-{self.seed}.pth"))
      torch.save(self.qf_1.state_dict(),
                 os.path.join(self.save_folder, f"ra-qf_1-{self.exp_name}-{indicator}-{self.seed}.pth"))
      torch.save(self.qf_2.state_dict(),
                 os.path.join(self.save_folder, f"ra-qf_2-{self.exp_name}-{indicator}-{self.seed}.pth"))
findings.claim: The training drivers only `learn(...)` then `save(...)` model weights; there is no function or script that loads a trained agent and evaluates it over 100 episodes to produce the mean ± standard-error returns reported in Tables 1, 2, and 3.
findings.concern: Tables 1–3 report returns 'tested over 100 episodes' with standard errors over '10 different seeds', but no code performs this held-out evaluation or aggregates seeds/standard errors, so the reported numbers cannot be regenerated from the repo.
findings.resolution: Authors: please add the evaluation harness (deterministic 100-episode rollout, per-seed aggregation, standard-error computation) that produced Tables 1–3.
findings.paper_ref: Tables 1–3 captions ('tested over 100 episodes ... mean ± standard error')
findings.reason: Algorithms.py lines 252–258 confirm only weight-saving; a repo-wide grep for 'evaluate', 'eval.*episode', 'torch.load', and 'load.*checkpoint' returns no hits, and the repo root contains no evaluation script.

findings.title: Table 3 sampling-weight ablation (α and weight on/off) not implemented
findings.quote:
# calculate the return weights: weights = softmax(1 / tail_returns)
return_weights = np.exp(1 / tail_returns) / np.sum(np.exp(1 / tail_returns))
# calculate the feature weights: weights = softmax(1 / sim_i), sim_i = c dot f_i / sqrt(dim(c)), c = mean(f)
center_feature = np.mean(tail_features, axis=0)
feature_sim = center_feature @ tail_features.T / math.sqrt(len(center_feature))
feature_weights = np.exp(1 / feature_sim) / np.sum(np.exp(1 / feature_sim))

weights = 0.5 * (return_weights + feature_weights)

pa_batch_size = (self.batch_size * weights).astype(int)
findings.claim: The balance factor between the performance weight (return_weights) and the similarity weight (feature_weights) is hard-coded to 0.5; there is no α argument, no code path for α=0.25/0.75, and no way to disable either weight (w/o wsim => α=0; w/o wper => α=1; w/o both => uniform).
findings.concern: Table 3 reports an ablation over α∈{0,0.25,0.5,0.75,1} and over removing each sampling weight, but the only configuration the code can run is α=0.5 with both weights on, so the ablation rows cannot be reproduced from the repo.
findings.resolution: Authors: please expose the α balance factor (and switches for w/o wsim / w/o wper / w/o both) as configurable parameters, matching the variants in Table 3.
findings.reason: Algorithms.py line 189 hard-codes `weights = 0.5 * (return_weights + feature_weights)` with no α parameter or conditional branches for disabling either weight term, exactly as claimed.

findings.title: No driver for the new-task transfer experiments (Table 2, Fig. 4)
findings.quote:
env_ids = ["MiniGrid-Type1", "MiniGrid-Type2", "MiniGrid-Type3", "MiniGrid-Type4"]
# env_ids = ["MiniGrid-Type5"]  # for the new task
findings.claim: The 'new task' transfer setting exists only as a commented-out single-task env line; there is no script that loads a pre-trained CRA, initialises a fresh policy agent on the held-out task, and runs the 'CenRA w/ learning' vs 'CenRA w/o learning' (frozen-CRA) conditions reported in Table 2 / Figure 4a.
findings.concern: Section 5.2 / Table 2 / Figure 4 are central to the paper's transferability claim, but reproducing them requires loading a saved CRA into a new run with the CRA optionally frozen — a code path that does not exist (the training algorithms always optimise the CRA and never load it from disk).
findings.resolution: Authors: please add the transfer-experiment script that loads the trained CRA checkpoint and runs the frozen-CRA ('w/o learning') and continued-learning ('w/ learning') conditions on the held-out tasks.
findings.reason: run-2dmaze.py lines 69–70 confirm the new-task env is a commented-out stub; no other script in the repo loads a CRA checkpoint or implements frozen-CRA vs continued-learning conditions.

findings.title: run-mujococar.py crashes: references undefined args.ra_buffer_size
findings.quote:
agent = CenRA_con(policy_agents=policy_agents, sample_env=envs[0], actor_class=RAActorVectorObs,
                  critic_class=RAQNetVectorObs, buffer_size=args.ra_buffer_size * len(envs),
                  batch_size=args.ra_batch_size, policy_lr=args.ra_actor_lr, q_lr=args.ra_critic_lr,
                  alpha_lr=args.ra_alpha_lr, policy_frequency=args.ra_policy_frequency,
                  alpha=args.ra_alpha, alpha_autotune=args.ra_alpha_autotune,
                  suggested_reward_scale=args.suggested_reward_scale, lamb=args.lamb)
findings.claim: Line 107 reads args.ra_buffer_size, but the argument parser in run-mujococar.py only declares --pa-buffer-size (no --ra-buffer-size). At runtime this raises AttributeError: 'Namespace' object has no attribute 'ra_buffer_size'. Verified by AST in _audit_code/check_mujococar_arg.py (ra_buffer_size is the only args.* access with no matching add_argument, and only in run-mujococar.py).
findings.concern: The MujocoCar experiment (a full column of Tables 1–3 and a Figure 3 panel) cannot be launched as shipped; the entry point fails before training begins.
findings.resolution: Authors: add a --ra-buffer-size argument (or change line 107 to args.pa_buffer_size) and confirm which buffer size was used for the reported MujocoCar runs.
findings.reason: grep confirms run-mujococar.py line 47 declares --pa-buffer-size but line 107 accesses args.ra_buffer_size; the AST check script (_verifier_code/check_mujococar_arg.py) executed and reported exactly this undefined access, while run-3dpickup.py and run-2dmaze.py are clean.

findings.title: CRA optimised with twin-Q SAC objective, not the single-V actor-critic of Eq. 1–2
findings.quote:
with torch.no_grad():
    next_shaped_reward, next_state_log_pi, _ = self.actor.get_action(data.next_observations, data.next_actions)
    qf_1_next_target = self.qf_1_target(data.next_observations, data.next_actions, next_shaped_reward)
    qf_2_next_target = self.qf_2_target(data.next_observations, data.next_actions, next_shaped_reward)
    min_qf_next_target = torch.min(qf_1_next_target, qf_2_next_target) - self.alpha * next_state_log_pi
    next_q_value = data.rewards.flatten() + (1 - data.dones.flatten()) * self.gamma * min_qf_next_target.view(
        -1)

qf_1_a_values = self.qf_1(data.observations, data.actions, data.shaped_rewards).view(-1)
qf_2_a_values = self.qf_2(data.observations, data.actions, data.shaped_rewards).view(-1)
qf_1_loss = F.mse_loss(qf_1_a_values, next_q_value)
qf_2_loss = F.mse_loss(qf_2_a_values, next_q_value)
findings.claim: The CRA is trained as a SAC agent with two soft Q-functions (twin critics, entropy term -alpha*log_pi, min over qf_1/qf_2, soft target updates, action = shaped reward). The paper's Eq. (1)–(2) instead describe a single state-value critic V_rwd trained on the TD error delta_t = r_env + gamma*V(s') - V(s) and an actor updated by log pi(r_knw|s)*delta_t (a one-step actor-critic, no twin Q, no entropy term).
findings.concern: The CRA learning rule actually implemented (twin-Q soft actor-critic) differs from the actor-critic equations stated in the methodology; both are valid RL objectives, but a reader reproducing Eq. (1)–(2) literally would build a different algorithm than the one that produced the results.
findings.resolution: Authors: please reconcile Eq. (1)–(2) with the SAC implementation (e.g., update the equations to the actual twin-Q soft actor-critic, or clarify that the CRA uses SAC with the listed entropy/temperature terms).
findings.reason: Algorithms.py lines 205–216 show the exact twin-Q SAC update (two Q-networks, entropy term -alpha*log_pi, min over targets) as quoted; the discrepancy with the single-V actor-critic in Eq. (1)–(2) is confirmed by the code evidence.
